summary: This advice column tackles three substantive questions and one playful one. A math tutor struggles with administrators who prioritize attendance numbers over effective teaching methods that require student participation. A woman asks how to handle sexist attacks in online forums, receiving advice to avoid toxic spaces entirely and never engage with identified assholes. The column also humorously addresses questions about sexy knitwear and women-friendly erotica, showcasing the author's irreverent range from serious gender issues to lighthearted topics.
key_points: Effective teaching that requires active student participation may conflict with institutional metrics that reward passive attendance numbers
Online anonymity enables hateful behavior, and the best response to sexist attacks is complete disengagement rather than continued interaction
Comment moderation is a necessary but burdensome investment to maintain quality discourse online
Women face a double standard in online spaces where they're targeted with gendered slurs for simply making intellectual points
The author advocates for finding curated spaces (online or offline) rather than tolerating toxic environments
money_quotes: If someone has identified themselves as an asshole, by saying something hateful and sexist to you after you try to make an intellectual point, please ignore them forever after.
What are you doing on discussion forums that allow such comments? Surely there are better things for you to do with your time. Like even just taking a nap would be a better use of your time.
It's a sad fact about anonymity of the web that there are weird, sad people who spend their time being hateful, as hateful as they can be, to as many people as they can be, via comments.
And if you tell me I'm being sexist because men still have the option to be on such forums without being called a cunt, I'd argue that anyone there is wasting their time.
Am I too low on the food-chain to be this principled about how to run a supplementary problem solving session for a single lower division math class?
I've had to go from allowing comments to moderating all mathbabe comments because of this problem, and it's a total pain in the ass, but the only other option is frankly to forbid all comments.
key_insight: The best response to online harassment is not engagement or even dignified silence within the space, but complete withdrawal from toxic environments combined with permanent dismissal of anyone who reveals themselves to be arguing in bad faith.
